summary: Luu critiques the common "must-read programming books" lists as nonsense given the field's breadth and diverse learning styles, instead offering nuanced recommendations based on actual reading experience. He systematically covers algorithms, operating systems, computer architecture, game theory, and hardware fundamentals, providing clear guidance on when to read comprehensive textbooks versus practical introductions. Throughout, he debunks the myth that certain "classic" books like CLRS or Peopleware are universally appropriate, arguing that many widely-recommended texts are impractical for self-study or mismatched to their purported audience.
key_points: Generic "must-read" lists fail because programming is too broad for universal requirements and learning preferences vary drastically
Many widely-recommended "classic" textbooks (CLRS, Kleinberg & Tardos, Peopleware) are impractical for self-study despite appearing on must-read lists
Understanding lower-level systems (OS internals, computer architecture) provides debugging superpowers and prevents massive performance waste in production systems
Textbook quality often inversely correlates with edition churn designed to extract money from students
Real learning value comes from understanding tradeoffs and developing intuition, not from memorizing formulas or achieving comprehensive coverage
money_quotes: The field is too broad for almost any topic to be required reading for all programmers, and even if a topic is that important, people's learning preferences differ too much for any book on that topic to be the best book on the topic for all people.

This book somehow manages to make it into half of these 'N books all programmers must read' lists despite being so comprehensive and rigorous that almost no practitioners actually read the entire thing.

I can think of multiple production systems at large companies that are giving up 10x to 100x in performance which are operating at a scale where even a 2x difference in performance could pay a VP's salary -- all because people didn't think through the performance implications of their design.

Modern computing is too complicated for any human to have a real full-stack understanding of what's going on. In fact, one reason modern computing can accomplish what it does is that it's possible to be productive without having a deep understanding of much of the stack that sits below the level you're operating at.
From a pure ROI perspective, I doubt learning math is 'worth it' for 99% of jobs out there.
key_insight: The best technical books teach you how to reason about tradeoffs and develop intuition rather than achieve encyclopedic coverage, and the most widely-recommended "classics" are often terrible choices for self-study despite their prestige.
